Tomato leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
Maintain proper spacing for air circulation
prevention: Water at soil level, not on leaves
Remove any yellowing leaves promptly

Tomato leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Tomato Early blight leaf.symptoms: Dark brown concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older/lower leaves. Yellow halo around spots. Premature leaf drop.
Tomato Early blight leaf.spread: Spreads via wind, rain splash, and infected soil debris. Favours warm humid weather (24-29°C).

organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately
Neem oil spray — 5ml per litre of water, spray every 7 days
Baking soda spray — 1 tsp per litre water + few drops dish soap
Mulch soil surface to reduce rain splash-back
chemical: Spray every 10-14 days, alternating chemicals to avoid resistance
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation — water at soil level
Crop rotation — do not plant tomato in same soil for 2 years
Use certified disease-free seeds
Maintain 45-60cm spacing between plants for airflow

Tomato Early blight leaf.urgency: Treat within 48 hours to prevent spread to upper canopy.

Tomato late blight leaf.symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked dark patches on leaves and stems. White fuzzy mould on underside of leaves in humid conditions. Rapid browning and collapse.
Tomato late blight leaf.spread: Extremely fast spreading. Spreads via wind-borne spores. Favours cool wet weather (10-20°C with high humidity).

organic: Remove ALL infected plant material immediately — bag and discard, do not compost
Copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture) — 4g per litre
Improve air circulation by pruning lower leaves
chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb — 2.5g per litre (systemic, works after infection)
Spray every 5-7 days during outbreak conditions
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g. Mountain Magic, Defiant)
Never plant tomatoes near potatoes
Avoid evening watering — leaves must dry before nightfall
Scout field every 2-3 days during cool wet spells

Tomato late blight leaf.urgency: URGENT — Late blight can destroy an entire crop in 7-10 days. Start treatment TODAY.

Tomato leaf bacterial spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown with yellow halo. Spots may have greasy appearance. Affects leaves, stems and fruit.
Tomato leaf bacterial spot.spread: Spreads through rain splash, wind, contaminated tools and hands. Entry through wounds and stomata.

organic: Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Copper-based spray (Bordeaux mixture) — 4g per litre every 7 days
Avoid working with plants when wet
chemical: Streptomycin sulphate — follow label (use sparingly to avoid resistance)
prevention: Use pathogen-free certified seeds
Sanitise tools with 10% bleach solution between plants
Avoid overhead irrigation
3-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops

Tomato leaf bacterial spot.urgency: Treat within 3 days. Bacterial diseases spread rapidly in wet conditions.

Tomato leaf mosaic virus.pathogen: Tomato Mosaic Virus / Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV/ToMV)
Tomato leaf mosaic virus.symptoms: Mottled light and dark green mosaic pattern on leaves. Leaves may be distorted, curled or reduced in size. Stunted plant growth.
Tomato leaf mosaic virus.spread: Spreads through contact — contaminated hands, tools, clothing. Also seed-transmitted.

organic: No cure — infected plants must be removed and destroyed
Wash hands thoroughly with soap before handling plants
Milk spray (10% dilution) — reduces virus transmission on tools
chemical: No effective chemical treatment for viral diseases
Imidacloprid can control aphid vectors that spread virus
prevention: Use TMV-resistant varieties
Do not smoke near plants — tobacco can carry TMV
Sanitise all tools before use
Control aphids and whiteflies — they spread viruses

Tomato leaf mosaic virus.urgency: Remove infected plants immediately. No treatment — focus on preventing spread.

Tomato leaf yellow virus.pathogen: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus — TYLCV (transmitted by whiteflies)
Tomato leaf yellow virus.symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing of leaf margins. Leaves become small and cupped. Severe stunting of entire plant. Flowers may drop without setting fruit.
Tomato leaf yellow virus.spread: Transmitted exclusively by silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Cannot spread by contact.

organic: Remove and destroy severely infected plants
Yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce whitefly population
Neem oil spray — 5ml per litre every 5-7 days to repel whiteflies
Reflective silver mulch on soil to confuse whiteflies
chemical: Spiromesifen — for whitefly nymphs on leaf underside
Target whiteflies — treating the virus directly is not possible
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties (e.g. TY-Zippel, Shanty)
Use fine mesh insect nets in nursery stage
Scout regularly for whiteflies on leaf undersides
Rogue out infected plants early before whitefly population builds

Tomato leaf yellow virus.urgency: Control whiteflies immediately. Once plant is severely infected, remove it.

Tomato mold leaf.symptoms: Pale green or yellow patches on upper leaf surface. Olive-green to grey-brown velvety mould on lower leaf surface. Older leaves affected first.
Tomato mold leaf.spread: Spreads via airborne spores. Thrives in high humidity (>85%) and temperatures 20-25°C. Common in greenhouse/polytunnel crops.

organic: Reduce humidity by improving ventilation
Remove heavily infected leaves
Copper-based fungicide — 4g per litre every 10 days
prevention: Maintain humidity below 85% — ventilate greenhouses
Water early morning so plants dry during the day
Plant resistant varieties
Remove crop debris after harvest

Tomato mold leaf.urgency: Treat within 5 days. Control humidity first — fungicide alone is insufficient.

Tomato septoria leaf spot.symptoms: Numerous small circular spots with dark brown border and lighter grey-white centre on lower leaves. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centres. Rapid defoliation.
Tomato septoria leaf spot.spread: Spreads via rain splash and wind. Favours warm wet weather. Overwinters in infected plant debris.

organic: Remove infected lower leaves immediately
Copper fungicide (Bordeaux mixture) — 4g per litre every 7-10 days
Avoid wetting foliage when watering
prevention: Crop rotation — at least 2 years before replanting tomato
Stake plants and prune for good air circulation
Remove all plant debris at season end
Mulch around base to prevent soil splash

Tomato septoria leaf spot.urgency: Treat promptly. Septoria can cause complete defoliation if unchecked.

Tomato two spotted spider mites leaf.symptoms: Tiny yellow or bronze stippling on upper leaf surface. Fine webbing on leaf undersides in severe infestations. Leaves turn bronze, dry out and drop.
Tomato two spotted spider mites leaf.spread: Spreads rapidly in hot dry conditions. Blown by wind. Spreads on clothing and tools.

organic: Strong water jet on leaf undersides to dislodge mites
Neem oil spray — 5ml per litre every 5 days
Insecticidal soap spray — 5ml per litre
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) in greenhouse settings
chemical: Spiromesifen — specifically for mites, low resistance risk
Rotate between chemical groups to prevent resistance
prevention: Maintain adequate soil moisture — mites thrive in dry stress
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilisation
Remove weeds around crop that harbour mites
Scout leaf undersides with magnifying glass weekly

Tomato two spotted spider mites leaf.urgency: Spider mites multiply extremely fast in heat. Begin treatment within 24 hours.

Potato leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
Scout for early signs of blight weekly
prevention: Hill soil around stems
Ensure good drainage to prevent waterlogging

Potato leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Potato leaf early blight.symptoms: Dark brown concentric ring spots (target-board pattern) on lower/older leaves first. Yellow chlorotic halo around lesions. Premature defoliation reduces tuber yield.
Potato leaf early blight.spread: Spreads via airborne spores and rain splash. Favours warm temperatures (24-29°C) with alternating wet and dry periods.

organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves
Neem oil — 5ml per litre every 7 days
Copper-based fungicide — 4g per litre
chemical: Begin spray programme at first sign of disease
prevention: 3-year crop rotation — avoid potato and tomato in same ground
Use certified seed tubers
Hill soil well to protect tubers
Maintain balanced nutrition — avoid excess nitrogen

Potato leaf early blight.urgency: Treat within 48 hours. Early blight reduces tuber size and yield significantly.

Potato leaf late blight.symptoms: Large water-soaked dark lesions on leaves and stems. White cottony mould on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Infected stems turn black. Tubers develop reddish-brown rot.
Potato leaf late blight.spread: Devastating — can destroy entire fields in days. Wind-borne spores spread kilometres. Favours cool wet weather (10-20°C).

organic: Remove ALL infected haulm immediately — bury or burn
Bordeaux mixture — 4g per litre every 5-7 days as preventive
Do not leave infected tubers in field
chemical: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb — 2.5g per litre (systemic, use after infection)
Spray every 5-7 days in high-risk weather
prevention: Use blight-resistant varieties (e.g. Sarpo Mira, Cara)
Never plant near tomatoes
Destroy all volunteer potato plants
Monitor blight forecasting alerts (BlightWatch, CIPRA)

Potato leaf late blight.urgency: CRITICAL — This is the disease that caused the Irish Famine. Act TODAY. Notify nearby farmers.

Corn leaf blight.symptoms: Long elliptical tan/grey-green lesions (5-15cm) on leaves. Lesions have distinct cigar-shaped appearance with wavy grey-green margins.
Corn leaf blight.spread: Wind-borne spores. Favours moderate temperatures (18-27°C) with high humidity and extended leaf wetness.

organic: Remove heavily infected leaves in early stages
Improve air circulation by reducing plant density
chemical: Spray at first sign, focus on upper canopy
prevention: Plant resistant hybrid varieties
Crop rotation — at least 1 year break
Reduce tillage to bury infected crop residue
Scout from V6 stage onwards

Corn leaf blight.urgency: Treat before disease reaches ear leaf or above — that is the critical threshold.

Corn leaf gray spot.symptoms: Rectangular tan to grey lesions bounded by leaf veins. Lesions run parallel to leaf veins, giving a blocky appearance. Premature leaf death in severe cases.
Corn leaf gray spot.spread: Airborne spores from infected crop residue. Favours warm humid conditions with heavy dew.

organic: Improve field drainage and air circulation
Remove infected lower leaves early
chemical: Spray from VT (tasselling) to R2 (blister) stage for best ROI
prevention: Plant tolerant/resistant hybrids
Rotate with non-host crops (soybean, wheat)
Reduce continuous maize planting
Till to bury residue in high-pressure fields

Corn leaf gray spot.urgency: Spray before disease reaches the ear leaf. Economic threshold is 5% severity on ear leaf.

Corn rust leaf.symptoms: Small oval to elongated brick-red/brown pustules (uredinia) scattered on both leaf surfaces. Pustules rupture and release dusty reddish-brown spores.
Corn rust leaf.spread: Wind-borne spores from alternate hosts (Oxalis spp.). Favours cool to moderate temperatures (16-23°C) with high humidity.

organic: Rust-resistant varieties are the primary management tool
Remove heavily infected leaves if disease is early-stage
chemical: Spray at first sign before pustules rupture
prevention: Plant resistant hybrid varieties (most modern hybrids have partial resistance)
Scout weekly from V8 stage
Avoid late planting into cool humid conditions

Corn rust leaf.urgency: Treat if more than 50% of plants show pustules before silking. Less critical after grain fill.

Bell_pepper leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Maintain consistent watering
Scout weekly for pests and disease

Bell_pepper leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Bell_pepper leaf spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked spots that turn dark brown with yellow halo on leaves. Fruit develops raised scabby spots. Severe defoliation in wet conditions.
Bell_pepper leaf spot.spread: Rain splash, wind, contaminated tools and seed. Entry through stomata and wounds.

organic: Remove and destroy infected plant material
Copper-based spray — 4g per litre every 7 days
Avoid overhead irrigation
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed
Sanitise transplants before field planting
Avoid working in field when plants are wet
2-year rotation with non-solanaceous crops

Bell_pepper leaf spot.urgency: Treat within 3 days. Copper sprays work best as preventive — start before heavy infection.

Blueberry leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
Maintain soil pH 4.5-5.5 for optimal health
prevention: Prune for open canopy and air circulation

Blueberry leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Cherry leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Prune to maintain open canopy

Cherry leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Cherry Powdery Mildew leaf.symptoms: White powdery coating on young leaves, shoots and fruit. Infected leaves curl upward. New shoots may be stunted and distorted.
Cherry Powdery Mildew leaf.spread: Wind-borne spores. Unique among fungi — thrives in warm dry weather with cool nights. Does NOT require leaf wetness to infect.

organic: Neem oil — 5ml per litre every 7 days
Potassium bicarbonate — 5g per litre (highly effective)
Milk spray — 30% milk dilution in water every 7 days
Sulfur dust or wettable sulfur — apply in cool conditions
chemical: Spray every 10-14 days from bud break
prevention: Prune to open canopy and improve air circulation
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilisation — promotes susceptible lush growth
Plant resistant varieties where available
Remove infected prunings from orchard

Cherry Powdery Mildew leaf.urgency: Treat early — powdery mildew on cherry spreads rapidly during dry periods.

Grape leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Maintain vine training for good air circulation

Grape leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Grape leaf black rot.symptoms: Tan-brown circular lesions with dark border on leaves. Tiny black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) visible in lesion centres. Berries shrivel into hard black mummies.
Grape leaf black rot.spread: Spores released from overwintering mummies and infected canes during wet spring weather. Infection period lasts 2-3 weeks around bloom.

organic: Remove and destroy all mummified berries — this is the most critical step
Copper-based spray — 4g per litre at bud break and pre-bloom
chemical: Myclobutanil — follow label (excellent, apply from green tip to 4 weeks post-bloom)
Critical spray window: 2 weeks before to 4 weeks after full bloom
prevention: Remove ALL mummified berries during winter pruning — they are primary inoculum source
Prune for open canopy to reduce humidity
Avoid planting in frost pockets or poorly drained areas

Grape leaf black rot.urgency: Focus sprays around bloom — this is the highest-risk infection window.

Grape powdery mildew leaf.symptoms: White to grey powdery coating on leaves, shoots and berries. Infected berries split, exposing seeds. Musty odour from infected tissue.
Grape powdery mildew leaf.spread: Wind-borne spores. Favours warm dry weather with moderate humidity. Does NOT require free water for infection.

organic: Sulfur dust or wettable sulfur — most effective organic option
Potassium bicarbonate — 5g per litre
Neem oil — 5ml per litre
chemical: Begin at bud break and spray every 10-14 days through veraison
prevention: Shoot and leaf removal around clusters at berry set to improve air circulation
Avoid excess irrigation and nitrogen
Monitor degree-day models for infection risk windows

Grape powdery mildew leaf.urgency: Protect clusters from infection — berry infection before 10mm berry size causes most damage.

Peach leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Maintain open-vase tree shape for air circulation

Peach leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Peach leaf bacterial spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked spots on leaves that turn dark purple-brown. Centres of spots may fall out leaving shothole appearance. Fruit shows dark sunken spots.
Peach leaf bacterial spot.spread: Rain-driven bacteria spread from cankers in wet spring conditions. Enters through stomata and wounds.

organic: Copper-based dormant spray at leaf fall and green tip
Remove and destroy cankered wood during dormant pruning
chemical: Copper hydroxide — 3g per litre at pre-bloom
Oxytetracycline — follow label for in-season sprays
Spray during and after rain events
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g. Redhaven, Contender)
Avoid planting in sites prone to late spring frost damage
Prune to reduce humidity within canopy

Peach leaf bacterial spot.urgency: Spray after every significant rain event from shuck-split through harvest.

Raspberry leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Remove old floricanes after harvest to reduce disease pressure

Raspberry leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Soyabean leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Scout regularly for soybean rust, especially from R1 stage

Soyabean leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Squash Powdery mildew leaf.symptoms: White powdery circular patches on upper leaf surface. Infected leaves turn yellow and die. Reduces photosynthesis and fruit quality.
Squash Powdery mildew leaf.spread: Wind-borne spores. Thrives in warm dry weather with high humidity. Does not need wet leaves to infect.

organic: Baking soda spray — 1 tsp per litre + few drops dish soap every 5-7 days
Neem oil — 5ml per litre
Potassium bicarbonate — 5g per litre
Milk spray — 30% dilution every 7 days
chemical: Sulfur-based fungicide — avoid in temperatures above 32°C
prevention: Plant resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove infected leaves promptly
Good spacing — at least 90cm between plants

Squash Powdery mildew leaf.urgency: Treat when first patches appear — powdery mildew spreads quickly in summer conditions.

Strawberry leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Remove old/dead leaves to reduce disease inoculum

Strawberry leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Strawberry leaf scorch.symptoms: Numerous small irregular dark purple spots on leaf surface. Spots do not have distinct pale centres. Leaves take on scorched or burnt appearance.
Strawberry leaf scorch.spread: Rain splash dispersal. Overwinters in infected leaves. Favours wet weather.

organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves — especially old overwintered foliage
Copper-based fungicide — 4g per litre at early season
chemical: Begin spray at first leaf spot appearance
prevention: Renovate beds after harvest — mow leaves, remove debris
Avoid overhead irrigation
Plant resistant varieties
Rotate planting sites every 3 years

Strawberry leaf scorch.urgency: Treat at first sign. Severe infections reduce runner and fruit production.

Apple leaf.symptoms: Leaf looks healthy with no visible disease symptoms.

organic: Continue regular monitoring
prevention: Prune annually for open canopy and air circulation

Apple leaf.urgency: No action required. Continue regular monitoring.

Apple Scab Leaf.symptoms: Olive-green to dark velvety spots on upper leaf surface. Spots turn brown and irregular with age. Infected leaves curl and drop early. Fruit develops dark scabby lesions.
Apple Scab Leaf.spread: Ascospores released from infected fallen leaves in spring during rain. Primary infection period is green tip to petal fall.

organic: Collect and destroy fallen leaves in autumn — removes primary inoculum
Sulfur-based fungicide — apply every 7-10 days from green tip
Lime sulfur at dormant stage
chemical: Captan — follow label (excellent protectant from green tip)
Critical window: green tip to 2-3 weeks post-petal fall
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g. Enterprise, GoldRush, Redfree)
Collect and destroy ALL fallen leaves each autumn
Prune for open canopy and air movement
Use scab forecasting models (Mills Table) to time sprays

Apple Scab Leaf.urgency: Apply protectant fungicide before infection events. Post-infection kickback possible within 72 hours.

Apple rust leaf.symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surface with pale halos. Later, tubular structures (aecia) form on leaf undersides. Defoliation in severe cases.
Apple rust leaf.spread: Requires two hosts — cedar/juniper trees nearby release orange gelatinous spore masses during spring rain. Spores infect apple leaves.

organic: Remove cedar/juniper trees within 300 metres where possible
Sulfur-based fungicide at green tip
chemical: Myclobutanil — follow label (apply from pink through first cover)
prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties (e.g. Enterprise, Liberty, Redfree)
Remove galls from nearby juniper/cedar trees before spring
Apply preventive sprays from pink to first cover stage

Apple rust leaf.urgency: Spray preventively around pink bud stage — infection happens within 4 hours of spore landing on wet leaves.
